book.name: Isaiah
book.author: Isaiah son of Amoz, a prophet in Jerusalem
book.dateWritten: c. 740-680 BC (chapters 1-39); some scholars date chapters 40-66 to the exilic/post-exilic period
book.writtenFrom: Jerusalem, during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah
book.writtenTo: The kingdom of Judah, and prophetically to all nations and future generations

explainer.overview: Isaiah is the longest and arguably the most important prophetic book in the Old Testament. It spans the full range of prophetic themes: judgment on sin, the fall of nations, the promise of a coming Messiah, the suffering servant, and the ultimate renewal of all creation. The book is often called 'the Fifth Gospel' because of its detailed messianic prophecies -- from the virgin birth (ch.7) to the suffering servant (ch.53) to the new heavens and new earth (ch.65-66). Isaiah's poetry is among the most beautiful in all of Scripture.
explainer.historicalContext: Isaiah prophesied during one of the most turbulent periods in Israel's history (c. 740-680 BC). The northern kingdom of Israel fell to Assyria in 722 BC. Judah faced the same threat under the Assyrian kings Tiglath-Pileser III, Shalmaneser V, Sargon II, and Sennacherib. Isaiah advised kings Ahaz and Hezekiah during these crises. The book's later chapters look forward to the Babylonian exile and beyond, envisioning restoration, the servant of the Lord, and a new creation.
explainer.whyItMatters: Isaiah is quoted or alluded to more in the New Testament than any other Old Testament book. Jesus read from Isaiah at the start of his ministry (Luke 4:17-21), and the early church saw Isaiah 53 as the clearest Old Testament prophecy of Christ's death and resurrection. The book's vision of justice, peace, and a renewed world continues to inspire movements for social justice and hope.
keyThemes: The holiness of God and the sinfulness of humanity
Judgment on Judah and the surrounding nations
The remnant -- a faithful minority preserved through judgment
The coming Messiah (Immanuel, Wonderful Counselor, Prince of Peace)
The Suffering Servant who bears the sins of many
The new heavens and new earth

people.name: Isaiah
people.role: Prophet in Jerusalem, author of the book
people.bio: Isaiah served as a prophet for approximately 60 years (c. 740-680 BC), advising four kings of Judah. He was married to a prophetess and had at least two sons whose names were prophetic signs. He had direct access to the royal court and was one of the most eloquent writers in the Old Testament. Jewish tradition holds that he was martyred by being sawn in two during the reign of the wicked King Manasseh.
people.culturalContext: Isaiah was likely from an aristocratic family in Jerusalem, given his easy access to the king. He combined the roles of prophet, political advisor, and poet. His commission came through a dramatic vision of God's throne (chapter 6), which shaped his lifelong emphasis on God's holiness.

people.name: King Ahaz
people.bio: Ahaz was one of Judah's worst kings. When threatened by a coalition of Israel and Syria, he refused to trust God (despite Isaiah's counsel) and instead appealed to Assyria for help -- effectively selling Judah's independence to a foreign superpower. He also introduced pagan worship into the Temple, including child sacrifice.
people.culturalContext: Ahaz represents the opposite of faith: when offered a sign from God (the Immanuel prophecy of chapter 7), he refused it. His decision to ally with Assyria rather than trust God set the stage for Judah's eventual downfall.

people.name: King Hezekiah
people.bio: Hezekiah was one of Judah's best kings, implementing sweeping religious reforms and trusting God during the Assyrian crisis. When Sennacherib's army besieged Jerusalem in 701 BC, Hezekiah prayed and God miraculously delivered the city. However, he later foolishly showed Judah's treasures to Babylonian envoys, prompting Isaiah's prophecy of the future Babylonian exile.
people.culturalContext: Hezekiah's reign represents the high point of faith in Isaiah's lifetime. His trust in God during the Assyrian siege (chapters 36-37) stands in stark contrast to his father Ahaz's faithlessness.

people.name: The Suffering Servant
people.role: Prophetic figure who bears the sins of the people
people.bio: The Suffering Servant appears in four 'Servant Songs' (42:1-9, 49:1-13, 50:4-11, 52:13-53:12). This figure is chosen by God, brings justice to the nations, suffers rejection and violence, bears the sins of many, and is ultimately vindicated. Christians identify the Servant as Jesus Christ; Jewish interpretation has varied between identifying the Servant as Israel collectively, an individual messiah, or the prophet Isaiah himself.
people.culturalContext: The concept of vicarious suffering -- one person bearing punishment on behalf of others -- was virtually unknown in the ancient world. The Suffering Servant prophecy introduced a revolutionary idea: that God's chosen one would save not through military victory but through personal suffering and death.

places.name: Jerusalem/Zion
places.description: The capital of Judah and the center of Isaiah's prophetic ministry. Isaiah's vision of God occurred in the Temple, and many of his prophecies concern Jerusalem's judgment, preservation, and future glory.
places.culturalSignificance: Jerusalem is mentioned more in Isaiah than in any other prophetic book. The city represents both the current failure of God's people and the future hope of God's kingdom. Isaiah's vision moves from Jerusalem judged to Jerusalem restored to a new Jerusalem in a renewed creation.

places.name: Babylon
places.description: The great empire that would eventually conquer Judah and exile its people. Isaiah prophesied Babylon's rise (chapters 13-14, 39) and its ultimate fall long before these events occurred.
places.culturalSignificance: Babylon functions in Isaiah as both a historical empire and a symbol of all human power that opposes God. Its predicted fall (chapter 13-14, 21, 47) demonstrates that even the mightiest empires are under God's sovereign control.

places.name: Assyria
places.description: The dominant military superpower during Isaiah's lifetime, ruling from Nineveh. Assyria destroyed the northern kingdom of Israel in 722 BC and threatened Jerusalem under Sennacherib in 701 BC.
places.culturalSignificance: Assyria was feared throughout the ancient world for its military brutality -- impaling captives, skinning prisoners alive, and deporting entire populations. Isaiah called Assyria 'the rod of God's anger' (10:5) -- an instrument of divine judgment that would itself be judged for its cruelty.

timeline.event: Isaiah's vision of God in the Temple and prophetic call (chapter 6)
Syro-Ephraimite War; Isaiah counsels King Ahaz; Immanuel prophecy (chapter 7)
Assyria conquers the northern kingdom of Israel (Samaria falls)
Hezekiah becomes king of Judah and begins religious reforms
Sennacherib's Assyrian army besieges Jerusalem; God delivers the city (chapters 36-37)
Hezekiah's illness and recovery; Babylonian envoys visit (chapters 38-39)
Isaiah's ministry ends; tradition says he was martyred under King Manasseh
Babylon destroys Jerusalem and the Temple (prophesied in chapter 39)
Cyrus of Persia conquers Babylon (prophesied by name in 44:28-45:1)
Jesus reads from Isaiah 61 in the Nazareth synagogue (Luke 4:17-21)

explainer.summary: God puts Judah on trial: the nation is sick from head to toe with sin. Despite abundant religious rituals, their worship is hollow because justice is absent. God calls for genuine repentance.
explainer.contextInBook: The opening chapter sets the tone for the entire book: Judah is sinful, God is holy, judgment is coming, but redemption is possible.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Set apart, sacred, morally pure
wordStudies.meaning: Set apart, sacred, morally pure. The fundamental attribute of God in Isaiah.
wordStudies.significance: God's holiness is the standard against which Judah's sin is measured throughout the book.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of society
wordStudies.meaning: Justice, judgment, the right ordering of society. Includes both legal fairness and social equity.
wordStudies.significance: God demands justice -- not just religious ritual -- as the true expression of faithfulness to Him.

verses.text: The vision of Isaiah the son of Amoz, which he saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem, in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah.
verses.explainer: The opening verse identifies Isaiah's ministry spanning four kings -- roughly 60 years of prophecy during one of Judah's most turbulent periods.

verses.text:
Hear, heavens,
and listen, earth; for Yahweh has spoken:
“I have nourished and brought up children,
and they have rebelled against me.
verses.explainer: God calls heaven and earth as witnesses in a cosmic courtroom scene: Israel has rebelled against the God who raised them.

verses.text:
The ox knows his owner,
and the donkey his master’s crib;
but Israel doesn’t know,
my people don’t consider.”
verses.explainer: Even an ox knows its owner and a donkey its master's feeding trough, but Israel does not know or understand its God -- less perceptive than farm animals.

verses.text:
Ah sinful nation,
a people loaded with iniquity,
offspring of evildoers,
children who deal corruptly!
They have forsaken Yahweh.
They have despised the Holy One of Israel.
They are estranged and backward.
verses.explainer: The nation is weighed down with sin and has utterly forsaken the Lord -- every word intensifies the severity of the rebellion.

verses.text:
Why should you be beaten more,
that you revolt more and more?
The whole head is sick,
and the whole heart faint.
verses.explainer: The whole nation is sick from head to foot -- there is no healthy part left. The body metaphor shows how completely sin has corrupted the nation.

verses.text:
From the sole of the foot even to the head there is no soundness in it:
wounds, welts, and open sores.
They haven’t been closed, neither bandaged, neither soothed with oil.
verses.explainer: From the sole of the foot to the head, there is nothing sound -- only wounds, bruises, and infected sores that have not been treated.

verses.text:
Your country is desolate.
Your cities are burned with fire.
Strangers devour your land in your presence,
and it is desolate,
as overthrown by strangers.
verses.explainer: The land is desolate, cities burned, and foreigners devour the countryside before their eyes -- the consequences of rebellion are already visible.

verses.text:
The daughter of Zion is left like a shelter in a vineyard,
like a hut in a field of melons,
like a besieged city.
verses.explainer: Jerusalem is left like a besieged city -- isolated and vulnerable, like a temporary shelter in a vineyard after harvest.
Without God's mercy preserving a remnant, Judah would have been completely destroyed like Sodom and Gomorrah.

verses.text:
Hear Yahweh’s word, you rulers of Sodom!
Listen to the law of our God, you people of Gomorrah!
verses.explainer: Isaiah shockingly addresses Judah's leaders as 'rulers of Sodom' and its people as 'people of Gomorrah' -- the most offensive comparison possible.

verses.text:
“What are the multitude of your sacrifices to me?”, says Yahweh.
“I have had enough of the burnt offerings of rams,
and the fat of fed animals.
I don’t delight in the blood of bulls,
or of lambs,
or of male goats.
verses.explainer: God is sick of their sacrifices: the abundance of religious ritual without genuine repentance is meaningless and even offensive to God.

verses.text:
When you come to appear before me,
who has required this at your hand, to trample my courts?
verses.explainer: God asks who required them to trample His courts -- their very presence in the Temple, without changed hearts, is an affront.

verses.text:
Bring no more vain offerings.
Incense is an abomination to me;
new moons, Sabbaths, and convocations:
I can’t stand evil assemblies.
verses.explainer: Their offerings are vain, their incense detestable, their assemblies corrupt. God cannot endure their combination of worship and wickedness.

verses.text:
My soul hates your New Moons and your appointed feasts.
They are a burden to me.
I am weary of bearing them.
verses.explainer: God hates their religious festivals -- strong language showing that ritual divorced from righteousness provokes divine disgust rather than pleasure.

verses.text:
When you spread out your hands, I will hide my eyes from you.
Yes, when you make many prayers, I will not hear.
Your hands are full of blood.
verses.explainer: Even when they pray, God will not listen because their hands are covered with blood -- violence and injustice make prayer meaningless.

verses.text:
Wash yourselves, make yourself clean.
Put away the evil of your doings from before my eyes.
Cease to do evil.
verses.explainer: God calls for action: wash yourselves, make yourselves clean, stop doing evil. The change must be moral, not just ceremonial.

verses.text:
Learn to do well.
Seek justice.
Relieve the oppressed.
Judge the fatherless.
Plead for the widow.”
verses.explainer: Learn to do right, seek justice, defend the oppressed, take up the cause of the fatherless, plead the case of the widow -- concrete actions, not abstract principles.

verses.text:
“Come now, and let us reason together,” says Yahweh:
“Though your sins be as scarlet, they shall be as white as snow.
Though they be red like crimson, they shall be as wool.
verses.explainer: God invites: 'Come, let us reason together. Though your sins are like scarlet, they shall be white as snow.' This is grace in the midst of judgment.

crossReferences.connection: 'The blood of Jesus cleanses us from all sin' -- the New Testament answer to Isaiah's invitation.

thenVsNow.then: The offer to have scarlet sins made white as snow is one of the Bible's most powerful images of complete forgiveness.
thenVsNow.now: The offer to have scarlet sins made white as snow is one of the Bible's most powerful images of complete forgiveness.

verses.text:
If you are willing and obedient,
you shall eat the good of the land;
verses.explainer: If they are willing and obedient, they will eat the good of the land -- God offers a path back from judgment to blessing.

verses.text:
but if you refuse and rebel, you shall be devoured with the sword;
for the mouth of Yahweh has spoken it.”
verses.explainer: But if they refuse and rebel, they will be devoured by the sword -- the choice between obedience and rebellion has life-or-death consequences.

verses.text:
How the faithful city has become a prostitute!
She was full of justice; righteousness lodged in her,
but now murderers.
verses.explainer: The faithful city has become a prostitute: once full of justice and righteousness, now full of murderers -- a devastating transformation.

verses.text:
Your silver has become dross,
your wine mixed with water.
verses.explainer: Silver has become dross and wine mixed with water -- the valuable has been degraded, the pure diluted. The nation has lost its quality.

verses.text:
Your princes are rebellious, and companions of thieves.
Everyone loves bribes, and follows after rewards.
They don’t judge the fatherless,
neither does the cause of the widow come to them.
verses.explainer: The rulers are rebellious companions of thieves who love bribes and chase gifts, ignoring orphans and widows -- corruption at the highest level.

verses.text:
Therefore the Lord, Yahweh of Armies,
the Mighty One of Israel, says:
“Ah, I will get relief from my adversaries,
and avenge myself on my enemies;
verses.explainer: Therefore the Mighty One of Israel declares He will take vengeance on His enemies and turn His hand against His own people to purify them.

verses.text:
and I will turn my hand on you,
thoroughly purge away your dross,
and will take away all your tin.
verses.explainer: God will smelt away the dross and remove all impurities -- judgment serves a purifying purpose, not just a punitive one.

verses.text:
I will restore your judges as at the first,
and your counselors as at the beginning.
Afterward you shall be called ‘The city of righteousness,
a faithful town.’
verses.explainer: After purification, Jerusalem will be called the 'City of Righteousness, the Faithful City' -- restored to what it was meant to be.

verses.text:
Zion shall be redeemed with justice,
and her converts with righteousness.
verses.explainer: Zion will be redeemed through justice and her repentant ones through righteousness -- redemption comes through God's justice, not human merit.

verses.text:
But the destruction of transgressors and sinners shall be together,
and those who forsake Yahweh shall be consumed.
verses.explainer: But rebels and sinners will be crushed together, and those who forsake the Lord will perish -- there are only two destinies.

verses.text:
For they shall be ashamed of the oaks which you have desired,
and you shall be confounded for the gardens that you have chosen.
verses.explainer: They will be ashamed of the sacred oaks and gardens where they practiced pagan worship -- the very places of their sin will become sources of shame.

verses.text:
For you shall be as an oak whose leaf fades,
and as a garden that has no water.
verses.explainer: They will be like an oak with withered leaves and a garden without water -- the things they trusted in will fail them.

verses.text:
The strong will be like tinder,
and his work like a spark.
They will both burn together,
and no one will quench them.”
verses.explainer: The mighty will become tinder and their work a spark -- both will burn together with no one to extinguish the fire. Sin consumes its practitioners.

explainer.summary: Isaiah envisions a future where all nations stream to God's mountain for instruction and peace. But first, Judah must face judgment for its arrogance and idolatry.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 2 introduces the pattern that runs through Isaiah: ultimate hope framed by present judgment.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of soci
wordStudies.meaning: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of society.
wordStudies.significance: God's judgment is not arbitrary punishment but the restoration of right order in a world corrupted by sin.

verses.text: This is what Isaiah the son of Amoz saw concerning Judah and Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.
Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Many peoples shall go and say,
“Come, let’s go up to the mountain of Yahweh,
to the house of the God of Jacob;
and he will teach us of his ways,
and we will walk in his paths.”
For out of Zion the law shall go out,
and Yahweh’s word from Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
He will judge between the nations,
and will decide concerning many peoples;
and they shall beat their swords into plowshares,
and their spears into pruning hooks.
Nation shall not lift up sword against nation,
neither shall they learn war any more.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text: House of Jacob, come, and let us walk in the light of Yahweh.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
For you have forsaken your people, the house of Jacob,
because they are filled from the east,
with those who practice divination like the Philistines,
and they clasp hands with the children of foreigners.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Their land is full of silver and gold,
neither is there any end of their treasures.
Their land also is full of horses,
neither is there any end of their chariots.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Their land also is full of idols.
They worship the work of their own hands,
that which their own fingers have made.
verses.explainer: Isaiah's satire against idol-worship exposes the absurdity of crafting a god from the same wood you use for cooking fires.
Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Enter into the rock,
and hide in the dust,
from before the terror of Yahweh,
and from the glory of his majesty.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
The lofty looks of man will be brought low,
the haughtiness of men will be bowed down,
and Yahweh alone will be exalted in that day.
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.
Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
For all the cedars of Lebanon, that are high and lifted up,
for all the oaks of Bashan,
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
For all the high mountains,
for all the hills that are lifted up,
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
For every lofty tower,
for every fortified wall,
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
For all the ships of Tarshish,
and for all pleasant imagery.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.
The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: The idols shall utterly pass away.
verses.explainer: Isaiah's satire against idol-worship exposes the absurdity of crafting a god from the same wood you use for cooking fires.

verses.text:
Men shall go into the caves of the rocks,
and into the holes of the earth,
from before the terror of Yahweh,
and from the glory of his majesty,
when he arises to shake the earth mightily.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
In that day, men shall cast away their idols of silver,
and their idols of gold,
which have been made for themselves to worship,
to the moles and to the bats;
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text:
To go into the caverns of the rocks,
and into the clefts of the ragged rocks,
from before the terror of Yahweh,
and from the glory of his majesty,
when he arises to shake the earth mightily.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Stop trusting in man, whose breath is in his nostrils;
for of what account is he?
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

explainer.summary: God removes Judah's leadership and social stability as judgment. The leaders have crushed the poor, and the wealthy women of Jerusalem flaunt luxury while others starve.
explainer.contextInBook: Isaiah targets specific social sins: corrupt leadership and callous wealth.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of soci
wordStudies.meaning: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of society.
wordStudies.significance: God's judgment is not arbitrary punishment but the restoration of right order in a world corrupted by sin.

verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
the mighty man,
the man of war,
the judge,
the prophet,
the diviner,
the elder,
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
the captain of fifty,
the honorable man,
the counselor,
the skilled craftsman,
and the clever enchanter.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
I will give boys to be their princes,
and children shall rule over them.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
The people will be oppressed,
everyone by another,
and everyone by his neighbor.
The child will behave himself proudly against the old man,
and the wicked against the honorable.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Indeed a man shall take hold of his brother in the house of his father, saying,
“You have clothing, you be our ruler,
and let this ruin be under your hand.”
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.
The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.
Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
The look of their faces testify against them.
They parade their sin like Sodom.
They don’t hide it.
Woe to their soul!
For they have brought disaster upon themselves.
verses.explainer: The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.

verses.text:
Tell the righteous “Good!”
For they shall eat the fruit of their deeds.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.
The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.

verses.text:
As for my people, children are their oppressors,
and women rule over them.
My people, those who lead you cause you to err,
and destroy the way of your paths.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Yahweh stands up to contend,
and stands to judge the peoples.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Yahweh will enter into judgment with the elders of his people,
and their leaders:
“It is you who have eaten up the vineyard.
The plunder of the poor is in your houses.
verses.explainer: The vineyard is Isaiah's metaphor for Israel: planted and tended by God, expected to produce justice and righteousness, but often yielding only bloodshed and oppression.

verses.text:
What do you mean that you crush my people,
and grind the face of the poor?” says the Lord, Yahweh of Armies.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Moreover Yahweh said, “Because the daughters of Zion are haughty,
and walk with outstretched necks and flirting eyes,
walking to trip as they go,
jingling ornaments on their feet;
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
therefore the Lord brings sores on the crown of the head of the women of Zion,
and Yahweh will make their scalps bald.”
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text: In that day the Lord will take away the beauty of their anklets, the headbands, the crescent necklaces,
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: the earrings, the bracelets, the veils,

verses.text: the headdresses, the ankle chains, the sashes, the perfume bottles, the charms,

verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text: the hand mirrors, the fine linen garments, the tiaras, and the shawls.

verses.text:
Your men shall fall by the sword,
and your mighty in the war.

verses.text:
Her gates shall lament and mourn;
and she shall be desolate and sit on the ground.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

explainer.summary: After judgment, a remnant will remain, and the Branch of the Lord will be beautiful and glorious. God will create a canopy of protection over Zion.
explainer.contextInBook: This short chapter provides a glimpse of hope beyond the judgment described in chapters 2-3.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of soci
wordStudies.meaning: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of society.
wordStudies.significance: God's judgment is not arbitrary punishment but the restoration of right order in a world corrupted by sin.

verses.text: Seven women shall take hold of one man in that day, saying, “We will eat our own bread, and wear our own clothing: only let us be called by your name. Take away our reproach.”
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: In that day, Yahweh’s branch will be beautiful and glorious, and the fruit of the land will be the beauty and glory of the survivors of Israel.
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: It will happen, that he who is left in Zion, and he who remains in Jerusalem, shall be called holy, even everyone who is written among the living in Jerusalem;
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text: when the Lord shall have washed away the filth of the daughters of Zion, and shall have purged the blood of Jerusalem from within it, by the spirit of justice, and by the spirit of burning.
verses.explainer: Justice (mishpat) in Isaiah is not abstract law but concrete protection of the vulnerable: the poor, the orphan, the widow, and the foreigner.

verses.text: Yahweh will create over the whole habitation of Mount Zion, and over her assemblies, a cloud and smoke by day, and the shining of a flaming fire by night; for over all the glory will be a canopy.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text: There will be a pavilion for a shade in the daytime from the heat, and for a refuge and for a shelter from storm and from rain.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

explainer.summary: The Song of the Vineyard: God planted Israel as a vineyard expecting good fruit, but it produced only bad grapes. Six 'woe' pronouncements follow, targeting specific sins of the people.
explainer.contextInBook: The vineyard metaphor is one of Isaiah's most powerful images, later used by Jesus in his parable of the tenants.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of soci
wordStudies.meaning: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of society.
wordStudies.significance: God's judgment is not arbitrary punishment but the restoration of right order in a world corrupted by sin.

verses.text:
Let me sing for my well beloved a song of my beloved about his vineyard.
My beloved had a vineyard on a very fruitful hill.
verses.explainer: The vineyard is Isaiah's metaphor for Israel: planted and tended by God, expected to produce justice and righteousness, but often yielding only bloodshed and oppression.

verses.text:
He dug it up,
gathered out its stones,
planted it with the choicest vine,
built a tower in the middle of it,
and also cut out a wine press therein.
He looked for it to yield grapes,
but it yielded wild grapes.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
“Now, inhabitants of Jerusalem and men of Judah,
please judge between me and my vineyard.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
What could have been done more to my vineyard, that I have not done in it?
Why, when I looked for it to yield grapes, did it yield wild grapes?
verses.explainer: The vineyard is Isaiah's metaphor for Israel: planted and tended by God, expected to produce justice and righteousness, but often yielding only bloodshed and oppression.

verses.text:
Now I will tell you what I will do to my vineyard.
I will take away its hedge, and it will be eaten up.
I will break down its wall, and it will be trampled down.
verses.explainer: The vineyard is Isaiah's metaphor for Israel: planted and tended by God, expected to produce justice and righteousness, but often yielding only bloodshed and oppression.

verses.text:
I will lay it a wasteland.
It won’t be pruned nor hoed,
but it will grow briers and thorns.
I will also command the clouds that they rain no rain on it.”
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
For the vineyard of Yahweh of Armies is the house of Israel,
and the men of Judah his pleasant plant:
and he looked for justice, but, behold, oppression;
for righteousness, but, behold, a cry of distress.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
Woe to those who join house to house,
who lay field to field, until there is no room,
and you are made to dwell alone in the middle of the land!
verses.explainer: The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.

verses.text:
In my ears, Yahweh of Armies says: “Surely many houses will be desolate,
even great and beautiful, unoccupied.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
For ten acres of vineyard shall yield one bath,
and a homer of seed shall yield an ephah.”
verses.explainer: The vineyard is Isaiah's metaphor for Israel: planted and tended by God, expected to produce justice and righteousness, but often yielding only bloodshed and oppression.

verses.text:
Woe to those who rise up early in the morning, that they may follow strong drink;
who stay late into the night, until wine inflames them!
verses.explainer: The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.
Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Therefore my people go into captivity for lack of knowledge.
Their honorable men are famished,
and their multitudes are parched with thirst.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.
Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.
Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
but Yahweh of Armies is exalted in justice,
and God the Holy One is sanctified in righteousness.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
Then the lambs will graze as in their pasture,
and strangers will eat the ruins of the rich.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Woe to those who draw iniquity with cords of falsehood,
and wickedness as with cart rope;
verses.explainer: The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.
Isaiah's distinctive title for God -- 'the Holy One of Israel' -- emphasizes both God's transcendent purity and His covenant commitment to His people.
The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.

verses.text:
Woe to those who are wise in their own eyes,
and prudent in their own sight!
verses.explainer: The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.

verses.text:
Woe to those who are mighty to drink wine,
and champions at mixing strong drink;
verses.explainer: The 'woe' oracle is a funeral cry -- Isaiah is already mourning those who will face God's judgment for their actions.

verses.text:
who acquit the guilty for a bribe,
but deny justice for the innocent!
verses.explainer: Justice (mishpat) in Isaiah is not abstract law but concrete protection of the vulnerable: the poor, the orphan, the widow, and the foreigner.

verses.text:
Therefore as the tongue of fire devours the stubble,
and as the dry grass sinks down in the flame,
so their root shall be as rottenness,
and their blossom shall go up as dust;
because they have rejected the law of Yahweh of Armies,
and despised the word of the Holy One of Israel.
verses.explainer: Fire in Isaiah serves as both judgment (destroying sin) and purification (refining the faithful) -- the same fire that consumes also cleanses.

verses.text:
Therefore Yahweh’s anger burns against his people,
and he has stretched out his hand against them, and has struck them.
The mountains tremble,
and their dead bodies are as refuse in the middle of the streets.
For all this, his anger is not turned away,
but his hand is still stretched out.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
He will lift up a banner to the nations from far,
and he will whistle for them from the end of the earth.
Behold, they will come speedily and swiftly.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.
Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
whose arrows are sharp,
and all their bows bent.
Their horses’ hoofs will be like flint,
and their wheels like a whirlwind.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
Their roaring will be like a lioness.
They will roar like young lions.
Yes, they shall roar,
and seize their prey and carry it off,
and there will be no one to deliver.
verses.explainer: Isaiah builds God's case against Judah: the nation has turned away from the Lord despite His faithful care, and judgment is the necessary consequence.

verses.text:
They will roar against them in that day like the roaring of the sea.
If one looks to the land behold, darkness and distress.
The light is darkened in its clouds.
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

explainer.summary: Isaiah sees the Lord high and lifted up in the Temple, with seraphim declaring 'Holy, holy, holy.' Overwhelmed by his own sinfulness, Isaiah is cleansed by a burning coal and responds: 'Here am I! Send me.'
explainer.contextInBook: This pivotal chapter records Isaiah's commission. The threefold 'holy' establishes God's transcendent holiness as the foundation of everything Isaiah will say.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Sacred, set apart, morally pure
wordStudies.meaning: Sacred, set apart, morally pure. The threefold repetition (holy, holy, holy) indicates the superlative -- God is infinitely holy.
wordStudies.significance: The trisagion establishes God's holiness as the foundation of everything Isaiah will proclaim.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Burning ones -- angelic beings associated with fir
wordStudies.meaning: Burning ones -- angelic beings associated with fire and God's purifying holiness.
wordStudies.significance: These beings appear only in Isaiah 6. They cover their faces (cannot look at God's full glory) and their feet (in humble reverence) while flying to serve.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Ritually or morally impure, defiled, polluted
wordStudies.meaning: Ritually or morally impure, defiled, polluted.
wordStudies.significance: Isaiah declares himself 'unclean' -- the same word used for leprosy and ritual impurity. In God's presence, even a righteous man is exposed as impure.

verses.text: In the year that king Uzziah died, I saw the Lord sitting on a throne, high and lifted up; and his train filled the temple.
verses.explainer: In the year King Uzziah died (740 BC), Isaiah saw the Lord seated on a high throne with the train of His robe filling the Temple -- divine glory replacing the dead king's authority.

verses.text: Above him stood the seraphim. Each one had six wings. With two he covered his face. With two he covered his feet. With two he flew.
verses.explainer: Seraphim (burning ones) hovered above God's throne, each with six wings: two covering their faces, two covering their feet, two for flying -- even angels shield themselves from God's full glory.

verses.text:
One called to another, and said,
“Holy, holy, holy, is Yahweh of Armies!
The whole earth is full of his glory!”
verses.explainer: The threefold 'Holy, holy, holy' declares God's supreme and infinite holiness -- the only attribute repeated three times in Scripture, indicating its absolute intensity.

culturalNotes.title: The Trisagion
culturalNotes.note: The threefold 'holy' (trisagion) became central to Jewish and Christian worship. In Hebrew, repetition intensifies meaning -- saying 'holy, holy, holy' means God is supremely, incomparably, infinitely holy.

crossReferences.connection: The four living creatures never stop saying 'Holy, holy, holy is the Lord God Almighty.'

verses.text: The foundations of the thresholds shook at the voice of him who called, and the house was filled with smoke.
verses.explainer: The doorposts shook at the sound of their voices and the Temple filled with smoke -- God's holiness literally shakes the building and fills it with His overwhelming presence.

verses.text: Then I said, “Woe is me! For I am undone, because I am a man of unclean lips, and I dwell among a people of unclean lips: for my eyes have seen the King, Yahweh of Armies!”
verses.explainer: Isaiah cries out in terror: 'I am ruined! I am a man of unclean lips living among unclean people, and my eyes have seen the King, the Lord Almighty!' -- seeing God's holiness reveals his own sinfulness.

verses.text: Then one of the seraphim flew to me, having a live coal in his hand, which he had taken with the tongs from off the altar.
verses.explainer: A seraph flew to Isaiah with a live coal taken from the altar -- the same fire that consumes sacrifice now purifies the prophet.

verses.text: He touched my mouth with it, and said, “Behold, this has touched your lips; and your iniquity is taken away, and your sin forgiven.”
verses.explainer: The burning coal touches Isaiah's lips: 'Your guilt is taken away and your sin atoned for.' Purification is God's initiative, not Isaiah's achievement.

verses.text:
I heard the Lord’s voice, saying, “Whom shall I send, and who will go for us?”
Then I said, “Here I am. Send me!”
verses.explainer: God asks 'Whom shall I send?' and Isaiah responds 'Here am I! Send me!' -- the most famous volunteer response in the Bible, born from an encounter with God's holiness and grace.

crossReferences.connection: The church at Antioch heard the Spirit say 'Set apart for me Barnabas and Saul' -- the same pattern of divine call and human response.

thenVsNow.then: Isaiah's response 'Here am I, send me' has become the model for answering God's call in every generation.
thenVsNow.now: Isaiah's response 'Here am I, send me' has become the model for answering God's call in every generation.

verses.text:
He said, “Go, and tell this people,
‘You hear indeed,
but don’t understand;
and you see indeed,
but don’t perceive.’
verses.explainer: God gives Isaiah a shocking commission: go tell this people to keep listening but never understand, keep looking but never perceive -- a message of judicial hardening.

verses.text:
Make the heart of this people fat.
Make their ears heavy, and shut their eyes;
lest they see with their eyes,
and hear with their ears,
and understand with their heart,
and turn again, and be healed.”
verses.explainer: Make the hearts of this people calloused, their ears dull, their eyes blind -- so they cannot repent and be healed. Jesus quoted this passage to explain Israel's rejection of his message.

verses.text:
Then I said, “Lord, how long?”
He answered,
“Until cities are waste without inhabitant,
and houses without man,
and the land becomes utterly waste,
verses.explainer: Isaiah asks 'How long, Lord?' The answer: until the cities are ruined, the houses empty, the land utterly desolate -- judgment must run its full course.

verses.text:
And Yahweh has removed men far away,
and the forsaken places are many within the land.
verses.explainer: The Lord will send the people far away and the land will be utterly forsaken -- the exile is prophesied, but it serves God's larger purposes.

verses.text:
If there is a tenth left in it,
that also will in turn be consumed:
as a terebinth, and as an oak, whose stock remains when they are felled;
so the holy seed is its stock.”
verses.explainer: Even when only a tenth remains and it is burned again, the holy seed is the stump -- devastation will be thorough but not total. A seed of hope survives.

explainer.summary: When Judah is threatened by Israel and Syria, Isaiah tells King Ahaz to trust God. Ahaz refuses, so Isaiah gives the Immanuel sign: 'A virgin will conceive and bear a son.'
explainer.contextInBook: The Immanuel prophecy has both an immediate context (a child born in Ahaz's time) and a messianic fulfillment (Jesus born of a virgin).

wordStudies.translatedAs: A sign, signal, or miraculous indicator from God c
wordStudies.meaning: A sign, signal, or miraculous indicator from God confirming His word.
wordStudies.significance: God offers Ahaz a sign as a tangible confirmation of His promise -- any sign at all -- but Ahaz refuses.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A young woman of marriageable age, typically imply
wordStudies.meaning: A young woman of marriageable age, typically implying virginity. The Greek translation used 'parthenos' (virgin).
wordStudies.significance: The Immanuel prophecy hinges on this word: a young woman/virgin will conceive. Matthew interprets it as fulfilled in Mary's virgin conception of Jesus.

verses.text: In the days of Ahaz the son of Jotham, the son of Uzziah, king of Judah, Rezin the king of Syria, and Pekah the son of Remaliah, king of Israel, went up to Jerusalem to war against it, but could not prevail against it.
verses.explainer: Ahaz faces a military crisis: the kings of Syria and Israel have formed an alliance to attack Judah and replace Ahaz with a puppet king.

verses.text: David’s house was told, “Syria is allied with Ephraim.” His heart trembled, and the heart of his people, as the trees of the forest tremble with the wind.
verses.explainer: The house of David shook with fear as trees shake in the wind -- the royal family and the nation are terrified of the coming invasion.

verses.text: Then Yahweh said to Isaiah, “Go out now to meet Ahaz, you, and Shearjashub your son, at the end of the conduit of the upper pool, on the highway of the fuller’s field.
verses.explainer: God sends Isaiah to meet Ahaz with his son Shear-Jashub (meaning 'a remnant shall return') -- the child's very name is a prophetic promise.

verses.text: Tell him, ‘Be careful, and keep calm. Don’t be afraid, neither let your heart be faint because of these two tails of smoking torches, for the fierce anger of Rezin and Syria, and of the son of Remaliah.
verses.explainer: Isaiah tells Ahaz to remain calm and not be afraid -- these two threatening kings are merely 'smoldering stumps of firewood,' about to burn out.

verses.text: Because Syria, Ephraim, and the son of Remaliah, have plotted evil against you, saying,
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: “Let’s go up against Judah, and tear it apart, and let’s divide it among ourselves, and set up a king within it, even the son of Tabeel.”
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: This is what the Lord Yahweh says: “It shall not stand, neither shall it happen.”
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: For the head of Syria is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin; and within sixty-five years Ephraim shall be broken in pieces, so that it shall not be a people;
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: and the head of Ephraim is Samaria, and the head of Samaria is Remaliah’s son. If you will not believe, surely you shall not be established.’”
verses.explainer: The climactic warning: 'If you do not stand firm in your faith, you will not stand at all' -- a play on words in Hebrew that links believing to surviving.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke again to Ahaz, saying,
verses.explainer: God speaks again to Ahaz, offering him an opportunity to ask for a confirming sign -- any sign at all, as dramatic as he wishes.

verses.text: “Ask a sign of Yahweh your God; ask it either in the depth, or in the height above.”
verses.explainer: The offer is extraordinary: 'Ask a sign from the Lord your God, whether in the depth below or in the height above' -- the entire range of possibility is open.

verses.text: But Ahaz said, “I will not ask, neither will I tempt Yahweh.”
verses.explainer: Ahaz refuses: 'I will not ask, and I will not put the Lord to the test.' This sounds pious but is actually defiance -- he has already decided to trust Assyria instead of God.

verses.text: He said, “Listen now, house of David. Is it not enough for you to try the patience of men, that you will try the patience of my God also?
verses.explainer: Isaiah's patience breaks: 'Is it not enough to try the patience of humans? Will you try the patience of my God also?' Ahaz's false humility tests God's own patience.

verses.text: Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign. Behold, the virgin will conceive, and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel.
verses.explainer: Therefore the Lord himself will give you a sign: the virgin will conceive and give birth to a son, and will call him Immanuel ('God with us'). This prophecy has both an immediate and a messianic fulfillment.

culturalNotes.title: The Virgin Birth Prophecy
culturalNotes.note: The Hebrew word 'almah' means a young woman of marriageable age, while the Greek translation (Septuagint) used 'parthenos' (virgin). Matthew 1:23 explicitly identifies this prophecy as fulfilled in Jesus's virgin birth.

crossReferences.connection: 'All this took place to fulfill what the Lord had spoken by the prophet: Behold, the virgin shall conceive.'

verses.text: He shall eat butter and honey when he knows to refuse the evil, and choose the good.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: For before the child knows to refuse the evil, and choose the good, the land whose two kings you abhor shall be forsaken.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: Yahweh will bring on you, on your people, and on your father’s house, days that have not come, from the day that Ephraim departed from Judah; even the king of Assyria.
verses.explainer: Assyria was the dominant military threat during Isaiah's lifetime, but Isaiah insists that Assyria is merely a tool in God's hand, not an independent power.

verses.text: It will happen in that day that Yahweh will whistle for the fly that is in the uttermost part of the rivers of Egypt, and for the bee that is in the land of Assyria.
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: They shall come, and shall all rest in the desolate valleys, in the clefts of the rocks, on all thorn hedges, and on all pastures.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: In that day the Lord will shave with a razor that is hired in the parts beyond the River, even with the king of Assyria, the head and the hair of the feet; and it shall also consume the beard.
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: It shall happen in that day that a man shall keep alive a young cow, and two sheep;
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: and it shall happen, that because of the abundance of milk which they shall give he shall eat butter: for everyone will eat butter and honey that is left within the land.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: It will happen in that day that every place where there were a thousand vines at a thousand silver shekels, shall be for briers and thorns.
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text: People will go there with arrows and with bow, because all the land will be briers and thorns.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: All the hills that were cultivated with the hoe, you shall not come there for fear of briers and thorns; but it shall be for the sending out of oxen, and for the treading of sheep.”
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

explainer.summary: Isaiah's son Maher-Shalal-Hash-Baz is born as a prophetic sign that Assyria will soon conquer Israel and Syria. The people must fear God, not foreign armies, and seek instruction from His word rather than from spiritists.
explainer.contextInBook: Isaiah's children serve as living prophecies -- their names embody God's messages to the nation.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of soci
wordStudies.meaning: Justice, right judgment, the fair ordering of society.
wordStudies.significance: God's judgment is not arbitrary punishment but the restoration of right order in a world corrupted by sin.

verses.text: Yahweh said to me, “Take a large tablet, and write on it with a man’s pen, ‘For Maher Shalal Hash Baz’;
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: and I will take for myself faithful witnesses to testify: Uriah the priest, and Zechariah the son of Jeberechiah.”
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: I went to the prophetess, and she conceived, and bore a son. Then Yahweh said to me, “Call his name ‘Maher Shalal Hash Baz.’
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: For before the child knows how to say, ‘My father,’ and, ‘My mother,’ the riches of Damascus and the plunder of Samaria will be carried away by the king of Assyria.”
verses.explainer: Assyria was the dominant military threat during Isaiah's lifetime, but Isaiah insists that Assyria is merely a tool in God's hand, not an independent power.

verses.text: Yahweh spoke to me yet again, saying,
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: “Because this people have refused the waters of Shiloah that go softly, and rejoice in Rezin and Remaliah’s son;
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: now therefore, behold, the Lord brings upon them the mighty flood waters of the River: the king of Assyria and all his glory. It will come up over all its channels, and go over all its banks.
verses.explainer: Assyria was the dominant military threat during Isaiah's lifetime, but Isaiah insists that Assyria is merely a tool in God's hand, not an independent power.

verses.text: It will sweep onward into Judah. It will overflow and pass through; it will reach even to the neck; and the stretching out of its wings will fill the width of your land, Immanuel.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: Make an uproar, you peoples, and be broken in pieces! Listen, all you from far countries: dress for battle, and be shattered! Dress for battle, and be shattered!
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: Take counsel together, and it will be brought to nothing; speak the word, and it will not stand: for God is with us.”
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: For Yahweh spoke thus to me with a strong hand, and instructed me not to walk in the way of this people, saying,
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: “Don’t say, ‘A conspiracy!’ concerning all about which this people say, ‘A conspiracy!’ neither fear their threats, nor be terrorized.

verses.text: Yahweh of Armies is who you must respect as holy. He is the one you must fear. He is the one you must dread.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: He will be a sanctuary, but for both houses of Israel, he will be a trap and a snare for the inhabitants of Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text: Many will stumble over it, fall, be broken, be snared, and be captured.”
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: Wrap up the testimony. Seal the law among my disciples.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: I will wait for Yahweh, who hides his face from the house of Jacob, and I will look for him.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: Behold, I and the children whom Yahweh has given me are for signs and for wonders in Israel from Yahweh of Armies, who dwells in Mount Zion.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text: When they tell you, “Consult with those who have familiar spirits and with the wizards, who chirp and who mutter:” shouldn’t a people consult with their God? Should they consult the dead on behalf of the living?
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: Turn to the law and to the testimony! If they don’t speak according to this word, surely there is no morning for them.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: They will pass through it, very distressed and hungry; and it will happen that when they are hungry, they will worry, and curse by their king and by their God. They will turn their faces upward,
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text: and look to the earth, and see distress, darkness, and the gloom of anguish. They will be driven into thick darkness.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

explainer.summary: Light breaks into darkness: a child is born who will reign on David's throne forever as Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace. But first, judgment comes on the northern kingdom.
explainer.contextInBook: The fourfold name of the messianic child is one of the most famous passages in all of Scripture, used prominently in Handel's Messiah.

wordStudies.translatedAs: A counselor whose wisdom is supernatural and mirac
wordStudies.meaning: A counselor whose wisdom is supernatural and miraculous -- beyond human comprehension.
wordStudies.significance: The first of four titles for the messianic child, emphasizing divine wisdom that surpasses all human advisors.

wordStudies.translatedAs: God the warrior, the divine hero
wordStudies.meaning: God the warrior, the divine hero. This title unambiguously identifies the child as deity.
wordStudies.significance: Applying the title 'Mighty God' to the child makes a stunning claim: this human child is also divine.

wordStudies.translatedAs: The ruler who brings and embodies complete well-be
wordStudies.meaning: The ruler who brings and embodies complete well-being -- wholeness, prosperity, harmony, and the absence of conflict.
wordStudies.significance: The final title establishes that the Messiah's kingdom will be characterized not by military conquest but by peace.

verses.text: But there shall be no more gloom for her who was in anguish. In the former time, he brought into contempt the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali; but in the latter time he has made it glorious, by the way of the sea, beyond the Jordan, Galilee of the nations.
verses.explainer: The land of Zebulun and Naphtali -- the region of Galilee, first to fall to Assyria -- will be the first to see a great light. Matthew applies this to Jesus beginning his ministry in Galilee.

verses.text:
The people who walked in darkness have seen a great light.
Those who lived in the land of the shadow of death, on them the light has shined.
verses.explainer: The people walking in darkness have seen a great light; on those living in the land of deep darkness a light has dawned -- one of Isaiah's most quoted messianic promises.

verses.text:
You have multiplied the nation.
You have increased their joy.
They rejoice before you according to the joy in harvest, as men rejoice when they divide the plunder.
verses.explainer: God has enlarged the nation and increased their joy, like the joy at harvest and the joy when soldiers divide plunder -- exuberant celebration.

verses.text: For the yoke of his burden, and the staff of his shoulder, the rod of his oppressor, you have broken as in the day of Midian.
verses.explainer: The yoke that burdened them, the bar across their shoulders, the rod of their oppressor -- God has shattered all of it, as in the day of Midian's defeat under Gideon.

verses.text: For all the armor of the armed man in the noisy battle, and the garments rolled in blood, will be for burning, fuel for the fire.
verses.explainer: Every warrior's boot and every garment rolled in blood will be fuel for the fire -- war itself will be consumed and destroyed.

verses.text: For to us a child is born. To us a son is given; and the government will be on his shoulders. His name will be called Wonderful, Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.
verses.explainer: For to us a child is born, to us a son is given, and the government will be on his shoulders. He will be called Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace.

crossReferences.connection: 'Today in the city of David a Savior has been born to you; he is the Messiah, the Lord.'
'In the beginning was the Word, and the Word was with God, and the Word was God' -- Mighty God incarnate.

thenVsNow.then: The four titles -- Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace -- capture the full scope of the Messiah's identity.
thenVsNow.now: The four titles -- Wonderful Counselor, Mighty God, Everlasting Father, Prince of Peace -- capture the full scope of the Messiah's identity.

verses.text: Of the increase of his government and of peace there shall be no end, on David’s throne, and on his kingdom, to establish it, and to uphold it with justice and with righteousness from that time on, even forever. The zeal of Yahweh of Armies will perform this.
verses.explainer: Of the increase of his government and peace there will be no end. He will reign on David's throne, establishing it with justice and righteousness forever.

verses.text:
The Lord sent a word into Jacob,
and it falls on Israel.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
All the people will know,
including Ephraim and the inhabitants of Samaria, who say in pride and in arrogance of heart,
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
“The bricks have fallen,
but we will build with cut stone.
The sycamore fig trees have been cut down,
but we will put cedars in their place.”
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
Therefore Yahweh will set up on high against him the adversaries of Rezin,
and will stir up his enemies,
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
The Syrians in front,
and the Philistines behind;
and they will devour Israel with open mouth.
For all this, his anger is not turned away,
but his hand is stretched out still.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
Yet the people have not turned to him who struck them,
neither have they sought Yahweh of Armies.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
Therefore Yahweh will cut off from Israel head and tail,
palm branch and reed, in one day.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
The elder and the honorable man is the head,
and the prophet who teaches lies is the tail.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
For those who lead this people lead them astray;
and those who are led by them are destroyed.
verses.explainer: During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.
During the Assyrian crisis, Isaiah delivers messages of both warning and messianic hope -- judgment is coming, but so is the promised deliverer.

verses.text:
For wickedness burns like a fire.
It devours the briers and thorns;
yes, it kindles in the thickets of the forest,
and they roll upward in a column of smoke.
verses.explainer: Fire in Isaiah serves as both judgment (destroying sin) and purification (refining the faithful) -- the same fire that consumes also cleanses.
