and he shall let my exiles go free,
not for price nor reward,” says Yahweh of Armies.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
Yahweh says: “The labor of Egypt,
and the merchandise of Ethiopia,
and the Sabeans, men of stature, shall come over to you,
and they shall be yours.
They will go after you.
They shall come over in chains;
and they will bow down to you.
They will make supplication to you:
‘Surely God is in you; and there is no one else.
There is no other god.
verses.explainer: Egypt represents the temptation to rely on human military power rather than trusting God -- a persistent sin of Judah's kings.

verses.text:
Most certainly you are a God who has hidden yourself,
God of Israel, the Savior.’”
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
They will be disappointed,
yes, confounded, all of them.
Those who are makers of idols will go into confusion together.
verses.explainer: Isaiah's satire against idol-worship exposes the absurdity of crafting a god from the same wood you use for cooking fires.

verses.text:
Israel will be saved by Yahweh with an everlasting salvation.
You will not be disappointed nor confounded to ages everlasting.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
For Yahweh who created the heavens,
the God who formed the earth and made it,
who established it and didn’t create it a waste,
who formed it to be inhabited says:
“I am Yahweh;
and there is no other.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
I have not spoken in secret,
in a place of the land of darkness.
I didn’t say to the offspring of Jacob, ‘Seek me in vain.’
I, Yahweh, speak righteousness.
I declare things that are right.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
“Assemble yourselves and come.
Draw near together, you who have escaped from the nations.
Those have no knowledge who carry the wood of their engraved image,
and pray to a god that can’t save.
verses.explainer: God's purposes in Isaiah extend far beyond Israel to encompass all nations -- His salvation is universal in scope.

verses.text:
Declare and present it.
Yes, let them take counsel together.
Who has shown this from ancient time?
Who has declared it of old?
Haven’t I, Yahweh?
There is no other God besides me, a just God and a Savior;
There is no one besides me.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
“Look to me, and be saved, all the ends of the earth;
for I am God, and there is no other.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
I have sworn by myself.
The word has gone out of my mouth in righteousness, and will not be revoked,
that to me every knee shall bow,
every tongue shall take an oath.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

crossReferences.connection: Paul applies this verse to Jesus: 'At the name of Jesus every knee should bow.'
Paul quotes this verse in the context of everyone giving account to God.

verses.text:
They will say of me,
‘There is righteousness and strength only in Yahweh.’”
Even to him shall men come;
and all those who raged against him shall be disappointed.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
All the offspring of Israel will be justified in Yahweh,
and will rejoice!
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

explainer.summary: Babylon's gods Bel and Nebo must be carried on beasts of burden, but God carries His people. He declares the end from the beginning and calls Cyrus a bird of prey from the east.
themes: Idols must be carried; God carries us
God declares the end from the beginning
explainer.contextInBook: The contrast between gods that must be carried and a God who carries His people is one of Isaiah's most powerful images.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.text:
Bel bows down.
Nebo stoops.
Their idols are carried by animals,
and on the livestock.
The things that you carried around are heavy loads,
a burden for the weary.
verses.explainer: This introduces a prophetic oracle -- a weighty message from God concerning the fate of a nation.

verses.text:
They stoop and they bow down together.
They could not deliver the burden,
but they have gone into captivity.
verses.explainer: This introduces a prophetic oracle -- a weighty message from God concerning the fate of a nation.

verses.text:
“Listen to me, house of Jacob,
and all the remnant of the house of Israel,
that have been carried from their birth,
that have been carried from the womb.
verses.explainer: The remnant theme is central to Isaiah: though judgment is severe, God always preserves a faithful minority through whom His purposes continue.

verses.text:
Even to old age I am he,
and even to gray hairs I will carry you.
I have made, and I will bear.
Yes, I will carry, and will deliver.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
“To whom will you compare me, and consider my equal,
and compare me, as if we were the same?
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Some pour out gold from the bag,
and weigh silver in the balance.
They hire a goldsmith,
and he makes it a god.
They fall down—
yes, they worship.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
They bear it on their shoulder.
They carry it, and set it in its place, and it stands there.
It cannot move from its place.
Yes, one may cry to it, yet it can not answer.
It cannot save him out of his trouble.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
“Remember this, and show yourselves men.
Bring it to mind again, you transgressors.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Remember the former things of old:
for I am God, and there is no other.
I am God, and there is none like me.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
I declare the end from the beginning,
and from ancient times things that are not yet done.
I say: My counsel will stand,
and I will do all that I please.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
I call a ravenous bird from the east,
the man of my counsel from a far country.
Yes, I have spoken.
I will also bring it to pass.
I have planned.
I will also do it.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Listen to me, you stubborn-hearted,
who are far from righteousness!
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
I bring my righteousness near.
It is not far off,
and my salvation will not wait.
I will grant salvation to Zion,
my glory to Israel.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

explainer.summary: The fall of Babylon personified as a pampered queen brought to ruin. Her sorcery and astrology cannot save her from the judgment God has decreed.
explainer.contextInBook: Babylon's fall is described with language later echoed in Revelation 17-18 about the fall of 'Babylon the Great.'

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.text:
“Come down, and sit in the dust, virgin daughter of Babylon.
Sit on the ground without a throne, daughter of the Chaldeans.
For you will no longer be called tender and delicate.
verses.explainer: Babylon will rise to destroy Jerusalem, but Isaiah prophesies that Babylon itself will fall -- no empire that opposes God's purposes lasts forever.

verses.text:
Take the millstones, and grind flour.
Remove your veil, lift up your skirt, uncover your legs,
and wade through the rivers.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Your nakedness will be uncovered.
Yes, your shame will be seen.
I will take vengeance,
and will spare no one.”
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Our Redeemer, Yahweh of Armies is his name,
is the Holy One of Israel.
verses.explainer: Isaiah's distinctive title for God -- 'the Holy One of Israel' -- emphasizes both God's transcendent purity and His covenant commitment to His people.

verses.text:
“Sit in silence, and go into darkness,
daughter of the Chaldeans.
For you shall no longer be called
the mistress of kingdoms.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
I was angry with my people.
I profaned my inheritance,
and gave them into your hand.
You showed them no mercy.
You laid a very heavy yoke on the aged.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
You said, ‘I will be a princess forever;’
so that you did not lay these things to your heart,
nor did you remember the results.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
“Now therefore hear this, you who are given to pleasures,
who sit securely,
who say in your heart,
‘I am, and there is no one else besides me.
I shall not sit as a widow,
neither shall I know the loss of children.’
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
But these two things shall come to you in a moment in one day,
the loss of children and widowhood.
They will come on you in their full measure,
in the multitude of your sorceries,
and the great abundance of your enchantments.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
For you have trusted in your wickedness.
You have said, ‘No one sees me.’
Your wisdom and your knowledge has perverted you.
You have said in your heart, ‘I am, and there is no one else besides me.’
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Therefore disaster will come on you.
You won’t know when it dawns.
Mischief will fall on you.
You won’t be able to put it away.
Desolation will come on you suddenly,
which you don’t understand.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.
God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
You are wearied in the multitude of your counsels.
Now let the astrologers, the stargazers, and the monthly prognosticators, stand up and save you from the things that will come on you.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Behold, they are like stubble.
The fire will burn them.
They won’t deliver themselves from the power of the flame.
It won’t be a coal to warm at
or a fire to sit by.
verses.explainer: Fire in Isaiah serves as both judgment (destroying sin) and purification (refining the faithful) -- the same fire that consumes also cleanses.

verses.text:
The things that you labored in will be like this:
those who have trafficked with you from your youth will each wander in his own way.
There will be no one to save you.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

explainer.summary: God reminds Israel that He predicted events long ago so they could not credit idols. He announces new things and calls Israel out of Babylon. If only they had obeyed -- their peace would have been like a river.
explainer.contextInBook: The lament 'If only you had paid attention to my commands' reveals God's grief over what could have been.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.text:
“Hear this, house of Jacob,
you who are called by the name of Israel,
and have come out of the waters of Judah.
You swear by Yahweh’s name,
and make mention of the God of Israel,
but not in truth, nor in righteousness
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.
God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.
God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.
God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.
Isaiah's satire against idol-worship exposes the absurdity of crafting a god from the same wood you use for cooking fires.

verses.text:
You have heard it;
see all this.
And you, won’t you declare it?
“I have shown you new things from this time,
even hidden things, which you have not known.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.
God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.
God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
For my name’s sake, I will defer my anger,
and for my praise I hold it back for you,
so that I don’t cut you off.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Behold, I have refined you,
but not as silver.
I have chosen you in the furnace of affliction.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
For my own sake,
for my own sake, I will do it;
for how would my name be profaned?
I will not give my glory to another.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
“Listen to me, O Jacob,
and Israel my called:
I am he;
I am the first,
I am also the last.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Yes, my hand has laid the foundation of the earth,
and my right hand has spread out the heavens.
when I call to them, they stand up together.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
“Assemble yourselves, all of you, and hear;
who among them has declared these things?
He whom Yahweh loves will do what he likes to Babylon,
and his arm will be against the Chaldeans.
verses.explainer: Babylon will rise to destroy Jerusalem, but Isaiah prophesies that Babylon itself will fall -- no empire that opposes God's purposes lasts forever.

verses.text:
I, even I, have spoken;
yes, I have called him.
I have brought him,
and he shall make his way prosperous.
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
“Come near to me and hear this:
“From the beginning I have not spoken in secret;
from the time that it happened, I was there.”
Now the Lord Yahweh has sent me,
with his Spirit.
verses.explainer: The Spirit of the Lord empowers God's chosen agents -- the messianic king, the Servant, and ultimately all of God's renewed people.

verses.text:
Yahweh,
your Redeemer,
the Holy One of Israel says:
“I am Yahweh your God,
who teaches you to profit,
who leads you by the way that you should go.
verses.explainer: Isaiah's distinctive title for God -- 'the Holy One of Israel' -- emphasizes both God's transcendent purity and His covenant commitment to His people.

verses.text:
Oh that you had listened to my commandments!
Then your peace would have been like a river,
and your righteousness like the waves of the sea.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
Your offspring also would have been as the sand,
and the descendants of your body like its grains.
His name would not be cut off nor destroyed from before me.”
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

verses.text:
Leave Babylon!
Flee from the Chaldeans!
With a voice of singing announce this,
tell it even to the end of the earth:
say, “Yahweh has redeemed his servant Jacob!”
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord is a key figure in Isaiah 40-55: sometimes identified with Israel collectively, sometimes with an individual who fulfills Israel's calling perfectly.

verses.text:
They didn’t thirst when he led them through the deserts.
He caused the waters to flow out of the rock for them.
He split the rock also, and the waters gushed out.
verses.explainer: Water imagery in Isaiah represents God's life-giving provision -- spiritual refreshment for those parched by sin and exile.

verses.text: “There is no peace”, says Yahweh, “for the wicked.”
verses.explainer: God comforts His exiled people with promises of return, revealing His sovereign power over all nations and the emptiness of all idols.

explainer.summary: The second Servant Song: the Servant was called before birth, formed in the womb for God's purpose. Though he seems to have labored in vain, God will make him a light to the nations. God has engraved Zion on the palms of His hands.
themes: The Servant's calling from the womb
explainer.contextInBook: The image of God engraving Zion on His palms is one of the most tender images of divine commitment in all of Scripture.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.text:
Listen, islands, to me.
Listen, you peoples, from afar:
Yahweh has called me from the womb;
from the inside of my mother he has mentioned my name.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
He has made my mouth like a sharp sword.
He has hidden me in the shadow of his hand.
He has made me a polished shaft.
He has kept me close in his quiver.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
He said to me, “You are my servant;
Israel, in whom I will be glorified.”
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord is a key figure in Isaiah 40-55: sometimes identified with Israel collectively, sometimes with an individual who fulfills Israel's calling perfectly.

verses.text:
But I said, “I have labored in vain.
I have spent my strength in vain for nothing;
yet surely the justice due to me is with Yahweh,
and my reward with my God.”
verses.explainer: Justice (mishpat) in Isaiah is not abstract law but concrete protection of the vulnerable: the poor, the orphan, the widow, and the foreigner.

verses.text:
Now Yahweh says, he who formed me from the womb to be his servant,
to bring Jacob again to him,
and to gather Israel to him,
for I am honorable in Yahweh’s eyes,
and my God has become my strength.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord is a key figure in Isaiah 40-55: sometimes identified with Israel collectively, sometimes with an individual who fulfills Israel's calling perfectly.

verses.text:
Indeed, he says, “It is too light a thing that you should be my servant to raise up the tribes of Jacob,
and to restore the preserved of Israel?
I will also give you as a light to the nations,
that you may be my salvation to the end of the earth.”
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord is a key figure in Isaiah 40-55: sometimes identified with Israel collectively, sometimes with an individual who fulfills Israel's calling perfectly.
The Servant of the Lord is a key figure in Isaiah 40-55: sometimes identified with Israel collectively, sometimes with an individual who fulfills Israel's calling perfectly.

verses.text:
Yahweh says, “In an acceptable time I have answered you,
and in a day of salvation I have helped you.
I will preserve you, and give you for a covenant of the people,
to raise up the land, to make them inherit the desolate heritage,
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
saying to those who are bound, ‘Come out!’;
to those who are in darkness, ‘Show yourselves!’
“They shall feed along the paths,
and their pasture shall be on all treeless heights.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
They shall not hunger nor thirst;
neither shall the heat nor sun strike them:
for he who has mercy on them will lead them.
He will guide them by springs of water.
verses.explainer: Water imagery in Isaiah represents God's life-giving provision -- spiritual refreshment for those parched by sin and exile.

verses.text:
I will make all my mountains a road,
and my highways shall be exalted.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
God's comfort is not mere sympathy but active intervention -- He is coming to restore, redeem, and renew His people.

verses.text:
But Zion said, “Yahweh has forsaken me,
and the Lord has forgotten me.”
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
“Can a woman forget her nursing child,
that she should not have compassion on the son of her womb?
Yes, these may forget,
yet I will not forget you!
verses.explainer: Songs of praise punctuate Isaiah's prophecy, reminding readers that God's ultimate purpose is not judgment but joy.

verses.text:
Behold, I have engraved you on the palms of my hands.
your walls are continually before me.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Your children hurry.
Your destroyers and those who devastated you will leave you.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Lift up your eyes all around, and see:
all these gather themselves together, and come to you.
As I live,” says Yahweh, “you shall surely clothe yourself with them all as with an ornament,
and dress yourself with them, like a bride.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
“For, as for your waste and your desolate places,
and your land that has been destroyed,
surely now that land will be too small for the inhabitants,
and those who swallowed you up will be far away.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
The children of your bereavement will say in your ears,
‘This place is too small for me.
Give me a place to live in.’
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Then you will say in your heart, ‘Who has conceived these for me, since I have been bereaved of my children,
and am solitary, an exile, and wandering back and forth?
Who has brought these up?
Behold, I was left alone. Where were these?’”
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Thus says the Lord Yahweh, “Behold, I will lift up my hand to the nations,
and lift up my banner to the peoples;
and they shall bring your sons in their bosom,
and your daughters shall be carried on their shoulders.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula signals that what follows is not Isaiah's personal opinion but God's direct word to His people.
Songs of praise punctuate Isaiah's prophecy, reminding readers that God's ultimate purpose is not judgment but joy.

verses.text:
Shall the plunder be taken from the mighty,
or the lawful captives be delivered?
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
I will feed those who oppress you with their own flesh;
and they will be drunk on their own blood, as with sweet wine.
Then all flesh shall know that I, Yahweh, am your Savior,
and your Redeemer, the Mighty One of Jacob.”
verses.explainer: God as Redeemer echoes the kinsman-redeemer concept: a close relative who pays the price to rescue a family member from slavery or debt.

explainer.summary: The third Servant Song: the Servant has the tongue of a disciple and gives his back to those who strike him, his cheeks to those who pull out the beard. He is not disgraced because God helps him.
explainer.contextInBook: This Servant Song increasingly reads like a preview of Jesus's passion: beaten, spat upon, but trusting God.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.text:
Yahweh says, “Where is the bill of your mother’s divorce, with which I have put her away?
or to which of my creditors have I sold you?
Behold, you were sold for your iniquities,
and your mother was put away for your transgressions.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Why, when I came, was there no one?
when I called, why was there no one to answer?
Is my hand shortened at all, that it can’t redeem?
or have I no power to deliver?
Behold, at my rebuke I dry up the sea.
I make the rivers a wilderness:
their fish stink, because there is no water, and die for thirst.
verses.explainer: God as Redeemer echoes the kinsman-redeemer concept: a close relative who pays the price to rescue a family member from slavery or debt.

verses.text:
I clothe the heavens with blackness,
and I make sackcloth their covering.”
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
The Lord Yahweh has given me the tongue of those who are taught,
that I may know how to sustain with words him who is weary.
He wakens morning by morning,
he wakens my ear to hear as those who are taught.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
The Lord Yahweh has opened my ear,
and I was not rebellious.
I have not turned back.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
I gave my back to those who beat me,
and my cheeks to those who plucked off the hair.
I didn’t hide my face from shame and spitting.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
For the Lord Yahweh will help me.
Therefore I have not been confounded.
Therefore I have set my face like a flint,
and I know that I shall not be disappointed.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
He who justifies me is near.
Who will bring charges against me?
Let us stand up together.
Who is my adversary?
Let him come near to me.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Behold, the Lord Yahweh will help me!
Who is he who will condemn me?
Behold, they will all grow old like a garment.
The moths will eat them up.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Who among you fears Yahweh,
and obeys the voice of his servant?
He who walks in darkness,
and has no light,
let him trust in Yahweh’s name,
and rely on his God.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord is a key figure in Isaiah 40-55: sometimes identified with Israel collectively, sometimes with an individual who fulfills Israel's calling perfectly.

verses.text:
Behold, all you who kindle a fire,
who adorn yourselves with torches around yourselves;
walk in the flame of your fire,
and among the torches that you have kindled.
You will have this from my hand:
You will lie down in sorrow.
verses.explainer: Fire in Isaiah serves as both judgment (destroying sin) and purification (refining the faithful) -- the same fire that consumes also cleanses.

explainer.summary: God calls Israel to look back to Abraham and Sarah (rock and quarry from which they were hewn) and forward to a future of comfort and redemption. Awake, awake, put on strength, O Zion!
explainer.contextInBook: The 'awake, awake' calls build momentum toward the great Servant Song in chapters 52-53.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.text:
“Listen to me, you who follow after righteousness,
you who seek Yahweh:
look to the rock you were cut from,
and to the quarry you were dug from.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
For Yahweh has comforted Zion.
He has comforted all her waste places,
and has made her wilderness like Eden,
and her desert like the garden of Yahweh.
Joy and gladness will be found therein,
thanksgiving, and the voice of melody.
verses.explainer: God's comfort is not mere sympathy but active intervention -- He is coming to restore, redeem, and renew His people.
Justice (mishpat) in Isaiah is not abstract law but concrete protection of the vulnerable: the poor, the orphan, the widow, and the foreigner.

verses.text:
My righteousness is near.
My salvation has gone out,
and my arms will judge the peoples.
The islands will wait for me,
and they will trust my arm.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
“Listen to me, you who know righteousness,
the people in whose heart is my law:
Don’t fear the reproach of men,
and don’t be dismayed at their insults.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
For the moth will eat them up like a garment,
and the worm will eat them like wool;
but my righteousness will be forever,
and my salvation to all generations.”
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
Awake, awake, put on strength, arm of Yahweh!
Awake, as in the days of old,
the generations of ancient times.
Isn’t it you who cut Rahab in pieces,
who pierced the monster?
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
God as Redeemer echoes the kinsman-redeemer concept: a close relative who pays the price to rescue a family member from slavery or debt.

verses.text:
Those ransomed by Yahweh will return,
and come with singing to Zion;
and everlasting joy shall be on their heads.
They will obtain gladness and joy.
Sorrow and sighing shall flee away.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
“I, even I, am he who comforts you.
Who are you, that you are afraid of man who shall die,
and of the son of man who will be made as grass?
verses.explainer: God's comfort is not mere sympathy but active intervention -- He is coming to restore, redeem, and renew His people.

verses.text:
Have you forgotten Yahweh your Maker,
who stretched out the heavens,
and laid the foundations of the earth?
Do you live in fear continually all day because of the fury of the oppressor,
when he prepares to destroy?
Where is the fury of the oppressor?
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
For I am Yahweh your God, who stirs up the sea,
so that its waves roar:
Yahweh of Armies is his name.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
I have put my words in your mouth,
and have covered you in the shadow of my hand,
that I may plant the heavens,
and lay the foundations of the earth,
and tell Zion, ‘You are my people.’”
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
Awake, awake!
Stand up, Jerusalem,
you who have drunk from Yahweh’s hand the cup of his wrath.
You have drunken the bowl of the cup of staggering,
and drained it.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
There is no one to guide her among all the sons to whom she has given birth;
and there is no one who takes her by the hand among all the sons who she has brought up.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
These two things have happened to you—
who will grieve with you?—
desolation and destruction,
and famine and the sword.
How can I comfort you?
verses.explainer: God's comfort is not mere sympathy but active intervention -- He is coming to restore, redeem, and renew His people.

verses.text:
Your sons have fainted.
They lie at the head of all the streets,
like an antelope in a net.
They are full of the Yahweh’s wrath,
the rebuke of your God.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Therefore now hear this, you afflicted,
and drunken, but not with wine:
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Thus says your Lord Yahweh,
your God who pleads the cause of his people,
“Behold, I have taken out of your hand the cup of staggering,
even the bowl of the cup of my wrath.
You will not drink it any more:
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
and I will put it into the hand of those who afflict you,
who have said to your soul, ‘Bow down, that we may walk over you;’
and you have laid your back as the ground,
like a street to those who walk over.”
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

explainer.summary: Awake, awake, put on beautiful garments, O Jerusalem! How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of those who bring good news. The Servant will act wisely and be raised and highly exalted -- but his appearance was marred beyond human likeness.
themes: Beautiful feet of good news bearers
explainer.contextInBook: Verses 13-15 begin the fourth and greatest Servant Song, which continues through chapter 53.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.text:
Awake, awake! Put on your strength, Zion.
Put on your beautiful garments, Jerusalem, the holy city:
for from now on the uncircumcised and the unclean will no more come into you.
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
Shake yourself from the dust!
Arise, sit up, Jerusalem!
Release yourself from the bonds of your neck, captive daughter of Zion!
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
For Yahweh says, “You were sold for nothing;
and you will be redeemed without money.”
verses.explainer: God as Redeemer echoes the kinsman-redeemer concept: a close relative who pays the price to rescue a family member from slavery or debt.

verses.text:
For thus says the Lord Yahweh:
“My people went down at the first into Egypt to live there:
and the Assyrian has oppressed them without cause.
verses.explainer: The prophetic messenger formula signals that what follows is not Isaiah's personal opinion but God's direct word to His people.

verses.text:
“Now therefore, what do I do here,” says Yahweh,
“seeing that my people are taken away for nothing?
Those who rule over them mock,” says Yahweh,
“and my name is blasphemed continually all day long.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Therefore my people shall know my name.
Therefore they shall know in that day that I am he who speaks.
Behold, it is I.”
verses.explainer: The phrase 'in that day' points to a future time when God will intervene decisively -- either in judgment or in salvation.

verses.text:
How beautiful on the mountains are the feet of him who brings good news,
who publishes peace,
who brings good news,
who proclaims salvation,
who says to Zion, “Your God reigns!”
verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

crossReferences.connection: Paul quotes this verse: 'How beautiful are the feet of those who bring good news!'

verses.explainer: Zion/Jerusalem represents both the historical city and the future dwelling place of God with His people -- judged for its sins but destined for glory.

verses.text:
Break out into joy,
sing together, you waste places of Jerusalem;
for Yahweh has comforted his people.
He has redeemed Jerusalem.
verses.explainer: God's comfort is not mere sympathy but active intervention -- He is coming to restore, redeem, and renew His people.

verses.text:
Yahweh has made bare his holy arm in the eyes of all the nations;
and all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our God.
verses.explainer: God's purposes in Isaiah extend far beyond Israel to encompass all nations -- His salvation is universal in scope.

verses.text:
Depart, depart, go out from there; touch no unclean thing!
Go out from among her!
Cleanse yourselves, you who carry Yahweh’s vessels.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
For you shall not go out in haste,
neither shall you go by flight:
for Yahweh will go before you;
and the God of Israel will be your rear guard.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Behold, my servant will deal wisely.
He will be exalted and lifted up,
and will be very high.
verses.explainer: The fourth Servant Song begins: the Servant will act wisely and be raised and highly exalted -- but what follows is the most devastating description of suffering in the Old Testament.

verses.text:
Just as many were astonished at you
(his appearance was marred more than any man, and his form more than the sons of men),
verses.explainer: His appearance was so disfigured beyond that of any human being, and his form marred beyond human likeness -- the suffering transforms him beyond recognition.

verses.text:
so he will cleanse many nations.
Kings will shut their mouths at him:
for they will see that which had not been told them;
and they will understand that which they had not heard.
verses.explainer: He will sprinkle many nations, and kings will shut their mouths because of him -- what they were not told, they will see; what they have not heard, they will understand.

explainer.summary: The fourth Servant Song: he was despised and rejected, a man of sorrows. He bore our griefs and carried our sorrows. He was pierced for our transgressions. By his wounds we are healed. The Lord laid on him the iniquity of us all.
explainer.contextInBook: Isaiah 53 is the most important messianic prophecy in the Old Testament. It describes in detail the suffering, death, and vindication of God's Servant, written 700 years before Jesus.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To pierce, to wound fatally, to profane
wordStudies.meaning: To pierce, to wound fatally, to profane. The word implies a violent, penetrating wound.
wordStudies.significance: The Servant is 'pierced' for our transgressions -- the wound is not accidental but deliberate, and it is for the sake of others.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To crush, to pulverize, to break into pieces
wordStudies.meaning: To crush, to pulverize, to break into pieces. Implies complete destruction of form and strength.
wordStudies.significance: The Servant is not merely hurt but utterly crushed -- the intensity of the word conveys the total nature of his suffering.

wordStudies.translatedAs: To heal, to make whole, to restore to health
wordStudies.meaning: To heal, to make whole, to restore to health. Covers physical, emotional, and spiritual restoration.
wordStudies.significance: The astonishing claim: through the Servant's wounds -- his suffering and death -- others receive healing and wholeness.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Guilt, punishment for sin, the crooked twisting of
wordStudies.meaning: Guilt, punishment for sin, the crooked twisting of what should be straight.
wordStudies.significance: The Lord has laid on the Servant the avon (guilt/punishment) of us all -- the entire weight of human sin is transferred to one person.

verses.text:
Who has believed our message?
To whom has the arm of Yahweh been revealed?
verses.explainer: 'Who has believed our message and to whom has the arm of the Lord been revealed?' The Servant's suffering is so unexpected that most people cannot believe it.

verses.text:
For he grew up before him as a tender plant,
and as a root out of dry ground.
He has no good looks or majesty.
When we see him, there is no beauty that we should desire him.
verses.explainer: He grew up like a tender shoot, like a root out of dry ground. He had no beauty or majesty to attract us -- the Messiah comes without outward grandeur.
He was despised and rejected by mankind, a man of suffering familiar with pain. Like one from whom people hide their faces, he was despised, and we held him in low esteem.

crossReferences.connection: 'He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him.'
At the crucifixion, people despised and mocked Jesus -- fulfilling this verse.

verses.text:
Surely he has borne our sickness,
and carried our suffering;
yet we considered him plagued,
struck by God, and afflicted.
verses.explainer: Surely he took up our pain and bore our suffering, yet we considered him punished by God, stricken by him, and afflicted -- we mistook his vicarious suffering for personal punishment.

verses.text:
But he was pierced for our transgressions.
He was crushed for our iniquities.
The punishment that brought our peace was on him;
and by his wounds we are healed.
verses.explainer: But he was pierced for our transgressions, crushed for our iniquities; the punishment that brought us peace was on him, and by his wounds we are healed.

crossReferences.connection: 'By his wounds you have been healed.'
'He was delivered over to death for our sins and was raised to life for our justification.'

thenVsNow.then: The concept of healing through another's wounds -- vicarious atonement -- is the theological heart of Christianity.
thenVsNow.now: The concept of healing through another's wounds -- vicarious atonement -- is the theological heart of Christianity.

verses.explainer: We all, like sheep, have gone astray, each of us has turned to our own way; and the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all -- universal sin met by substitutionary suffering.

verses.text:
He was oppressed,
yet when he was afflicted he didn’t open his mouth.
As a lamb that is led to the slaughter,
and as a sheep that before its shearers is silent,
so he didn’t open his mouth.
verses.explainer: He was oppressed and afflicted, yet he did not open his mouth; he was led like a lamb to the slaughter, and as a sheep before its shearers is silent, so he did not open his mouth.

crossReferences.connection: The Ethiopian eunuch was reading this exact passage when Philip explained that it referred to Jesus.
John the Baptist declared Jesus to be 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world.'

verses.text:
He was taken away by oppression and judgment;
and as for his generation,
who considered that he was cut off out of the land of the living
and stricken for the disobedience of my people?
verses.explainer: By oppression and judgment he was taken away. He was cut off from the land of the living; for the transgression of my people he was punished.

verses.text:
They made his grave with the wicked,
and with a rich man in his death;
although he had done no violence,
nor was any deceit in his mouth.
verses.explainer: He was assigned a grave with the wicked, and with the rich in his death, though he had done no violence, nor was any deceit in his mouth -- innocent, yet condemned.

verses.text:
Yet it pleased Yahweh to bruise him.
He has caused him to suffer.
When you make his soul an offering for sin,
he will see his offspring.
He will prolong his days,
and Yahweh’s pleasure will prosper in his hand.
verses.explainer: Yet it was the Lord's will to crush him and cause him to suffer. After he makes his life an offering for sin, he will see his offspring, prolong his days, and the Lord's purpose will prosper through him.

verses.text:
After the suffering of his soul,
he will see the light and be satisfied.
My righteous servant will justify many by the knowledge of himself;
and he will bear their iniquities.
verses.explainer: After his suffering, he will see the light of life and be satisfied. By his knowledge my righteous servant will justify many, and he will bear their iniquities.

Therefore he will be given a great portion among the great because he poured out his life unto death, was numbered with the transgressors, bore the sin of many, and made intercession for the transgressors.

explainer.summary: The barren woman will have more children than the married woman. God's covenant of peace will not be removed. No weapon formed against God's people will prosper.
explainer.contextInBook: After the Servant's suffering and death (ch.53), chapter 54 describes the abundant life that flows from his sacrifice.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Servant, slave, one who serves another
wordStudies.meaning: Servant, slave, one who serves another. Can denote honor (servant of the king) or humility (slave).
wordStudies.significance: The 'Servant of the Lord' is Isaiah's most important figure: called by God, empowered by the Spirit, and destined to save through suffering.

verses.explainer: Songs of praise punctuate Isaiah's prophecy, reminding readers that God's ultimate purpose is not judgment but joy.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
God's purposes in Isaiah extend far beyond Israel to encompass all nations -- His salvation is universal in scope.

verses.text:
“Don’t be afraid; for you will not be ashamed.
Don’t be confounded; for you will not be disappointed.
For you will forget the shame of your youth;
and the reproach of your widowhood you shall remember no more.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
For your Maker is your husband; Yahweh of Armies is his name.
The Holy One of Israel is your Redeemer.
He will be called the God of the whole earth.
verses.explainer: Isaiah's distinctive title for God -- 'the Holy One of Israel' -- emphasizes both God's transcendent purity and His covenant commitment to His people.

verses.text:
For Yahweh has called you as a wife forsaken and grieved in spirit,
even a wife of youth, when she is cast off,” says your God.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
“For a small moment I have forsaken you;
but with great mercies will I gather you.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
In overflowing wrath I hid my face from you for a moment;
but with everlasting loving kindness I will have mercy on you,” says Yahweh your Redeemer.
verses.explainer: God as Redeemer echoes the kinsman-redeemer concept: a close relative who pays the price to rescue a family member from slavery or debt.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
For the mountains may depart,
and the hills be removed;
but my loving kindness will not depart from you,
and my covenant of peace will not be removed,”
says Yahweh who has mercy on you.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
“You afflicted, tossed with storms, and not comforted,
behold, I will set your stones in beautiful colors,
and lay your foundations with sapphires.
verses.explainer: God's comfort is not mere sympathy but active intervention -- He is coming to restore, redeem, and renew His people.

verses.text:
I will make your pinnacles of rubies,
your gates of sparkling jewels,
and all your walls of precious stones.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
All your children will be taught by Yahweh;
and your children’s peace will be great.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
In righteousness you will be established.
You will be far from oppression,
for you will not be afraid;
and far from terror,
for it shall not come near you.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
Behold, they may gather together, but not by me.
Whoever gathers together against you will fall because of you.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
No weapon that is formed against you will prevail;
and you will condemn every tongue that rises against you in judgment.
This is the heritage of Yahweh’s servants,
and their righteousness is of me,” says Yahweh.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord is a key figure in Isaiah 40-55: sometimes identified with Israel collectively, sometimes with an individual who fulfills Israel's calling perfectly.

explainer.summary: 'Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters!' God's word will not return empty but will accomplish His purposes. The mountains and hills will burst into song, and the trees will clap their hands.
explainer.contextInBook: Chapter 55 is Isaiah's great altar call -- a free, universal invitation to come to God without cost.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Word, matter, thing
wordStudies.meaning: Word, matter, thing. In Hebrew, a word is not just sound -- it is an active force that creates reality.
wordStudies.significance: God's word goes out and accomplishes its purpose with the certainty of rain producing crops -- divine speech is always effective.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Thirsty, parched, longing for water
wordStudies.meaning: Thirsty, parched, longing for water. Used literally and as a metaphor for spiritual need.
wordStudies.significance: The invitation to 'everyone who thirsts' makes God's salvation available to anyone who recognizes their need.

verses.text:
“Hey! Come, everyone who thirsts, to the waters!
Come, he who has no money, buy, and eat!
Yes, come, buy wine and milk without money and without price.
verses.explainer: 'Come, all you who are thirsty, come to the waters; and you who have no money, come, buy and eat!' God's salvation is a free gift, available to all without cost.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus cried out: 'Let anyone who is thirsty come to me and drink.'
'Let the one who is thirsty come; let the one who wishes take the free gift of the water of life.'

verses.text:
Why do you spend money for that which is not bread,
and your labor for that which doesn’t satisfy?
Listen diligently to me, and eat that which is good,
and let your soul delight itself in richness.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Turn your ear, and come to me.
Hear, and your soul will live:
and I will make an everlasting covenant with you, even the sure mercies of David.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
Behold, I have given him for a witness to the peoples,
a leader and commander to the peoples.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.
Isaiah's distinctive title for God -- 'the Holy One of Israel' -- emphasizes both God's transcendent purity and His covenant commitment to His people.

verses.text:
Seek Yahweh while he may be found.
Call on him while he is near.
verses.explainer: 'Seek the Lord while he may be found; call on him while he is near' -- there is urgency in the invitation; the window of opportunity is not unlimited.

verses.text:
Let the wicked forsake his way,
and the unrighteous man his thoughts.
Let him return to Yahweh, and he will have mercy on him;
and to our God, for he will freely pardon.
verses.explainer: The Servant of the Lord emerges as God's answer to Israel's failure -- through the Servant's suffering and sacrifice, salvation comes to all nations.

verses.text:
“For my thoughts are not your thoughts,
and your ways are not my ways,” says Yahweh.
verses.explainer: 'For my thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways my ways,' declares the Lord -- God's plans transcend human understanding.

verses.text:
“For as the heavens are higher than the earth,
so are my ways higher than your ways,
and my thoughts than your thoughts.
verses.explainer: 'As the heavens are higher than the earth, so are my ways higher than your ways and my thoughts than your thoughts' -- the gap between divine and human wisdom is immeasurable.

verses.text:
For as the rain comes down and the snow from the sky,
and doesn’t return there, but waters the earth,
and makes it grow and bud,
and gives seed to the sower and bread to the eater;
verses.explainer: As rain and snow water the earth and make it produce, so God's word goes out and accomplishes its intended purpose -- divine speech is always effective.

verses.text:
so is my word that goes out of my mouth:
it will not return to me void,
but it will accomplish that which I please,
and it will prosper in the thing I sent it to do.
verses.explainer: God's word will not return empty but will accomplish what He desires and achieve the purpose for which He sent it -- a foundational promise for all who rely on Scripture.

crossReferences.connection: 'The word of God is alive and active, sharper than any double-edged sword.'

thenVsNow.then: The promise that God's word always accomplishes its purpose gives confidence to everyone who shares or teaches Scripture.
thenVsNow.now: The promise that God's word always accomplishes its purpose gives confidence to everyone who shares or teaches Scripture.

verses.text:
For you shall go out with joy,
and be led out with peace.
The mountains and the hills will break out before you into singing;
and all the trees of the fields will clap their hands.
verses.explainer: You will go out in joy and be led forth in peace. The mountains and hills will burst into song, and the trees will clap their hands -- all creation celebrates redemption.

verses.text:
Instead of the thorn the cypress tree will come up;
and instead of the brier the myrtle tree will come up:
and it will make a name for Yahweh,
for an everlasting sign that will not be cut off.”
verses.explainer: Instead of the thornbush, the juniper will grow, and instead of briers, the myrtle -- creation itself is transformed by God's redemptive work.

explainer.summary: God's salvation is for everyone, including foreigners and eunuchs previously excluded. God's house will be a house of prayer for all nations. But Israel's watchmen are blind and its shepherds greedy.
themes: House of prayer for all nations
explainer.contextInBook: Jesus quoted 'My house shall be called a house of prayer for all nations' when cleansing the Temple.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Glory, weight, honor, splendor
wordStudies.meaning: Glory, weight, honor, splendor. Literally 'heaviness' -- God's glory is the weighty reality of His presence.
wordStudies.significance: The final section of Isaiah builds toward the revelation of God's glory to all nations in the new creation.

verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
Blessed is the man who does this,
and the son of man who holds it fast;
who keeps the Sabbath without profaning it,
and keeps his hand from doing any evil.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Let no foreigner, who has joined himself to Yahweh, speak, saying,
“Yahweh will surely separate me from his people.”
Do not let the eunuch say, “Behold, I am a dry tree.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
For Yahweh says, “To the eunuchs who keep my Sabbaths,
and choose the things that please me,
and hold fast to my covenant:
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
I will give them in my house and within my walls a memorial and a name better than of sons and of daughters.
I will give them an everlasting name, that will not be cut off.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Also the foreigners who join themselves to Yahweh,
to serve him,
and to love Yahweh’s name,
to be his servants,
everyone who keeps the Sabbath from profaning it,
and holds fast my covenant;
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
I will bring these to my holy mountain,
and make them joyful in my house of prayer.
Their burnt offerings and their sacrifices will be accepted on my altar;
for my house will be called a house of prayer for all peoples.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
The Lord Yahweh, who gathers the outcasts of Israel, says,
“I will yet gather others to him,
besides his own who are gathered.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
All you animals of the field,
come to devour,
all you animals in the forest.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
His watchmen are blind.
They are all without knowledge.
They are all mute dogs.
They can’t bark;
dreaming, lying down, loving to slumber.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Yes, the dogs are greedy.
They can never have enough.
They are shepherds who can’t understand.
They have all turned to their own way,
each one to his gain, from every quarter.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
“Come,” say they, “I will get wine,
and we will fill ourselves with strong drink;
and tomorrow will be as today,
great beyond measure.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

explainer.summary: The righteous die and enter peace while the wicked continue in idolatry and child sacrifice. Yet God, who dwells in the high and holy place, also dwells with the contrite and lowly in spirit.
explainer.contextInBook: The paradox of God dwelling in both the highest heaven and with the lowliest person is one of Isaiah's most profound theological statements.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Glory, weight, honor, splendor
wordStudies.meaning: Glory, weight, honor, splendor. Literally 'heaviness' -- God's glory is the weighty reality of His presence.
wordStudies.significance: The final section of Isaiah builds toward the revelation of God's glory to all nations in the new creation.

verses.text:
The righteous perish,
and no one lays it to heart.
Merciful men are taken away,
and no one considers that the righteous is taken away from the evil.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
He enters into peace.
They rest in their beds,
each one who walks in his uprightness.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
“But draw near here, you sons of a sorceress,
you offspring of adulterers and prostitutes.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Whom do you mock?
Against whom do you make a wide mouth,
and stick out your tongue?
Aren’t you children of disobedience,
and offspring of falsehood,
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
you who inflame yourselves among the oaks,
under every green tree;
who kill the children in the valleys,
under the clefts of the rocks?
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Among the smooth stones of the valley is your portion;
they, they are your lot.
You have even poured a drink offering to them.
You have offered an offering.
Shall I be appeased for these things?
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
On a high and lofty mountain you have set your bed.
You also went up there to offer sacrifice.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.
True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
You went to the king with oil,
and increased your perfumes,
and sent your ambassadors far off,
and degraded yourself even to Sheol.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.
True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
“Whom have you dreaded and feared,
so that you lie,
and have not remembered me, nor laid it to your heart?
Haven’t I held my peace for a long time,
and you don’t fear me?
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
I will declare your righteousness;
and as for your works, they will not benefit you.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
When you cry,
let those whom you have gathered deliver you;
but the wind will take them.
a breath will carry them all away:
but he who takes refuge in me will possess the land,
and will inherit my holy mountain.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
He will say, “Build up, build up, prepare the way!
Remove the stumbling-block out of the way of my people.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
For thus says the high and lofty One who inhabits eternity,
whose name is Holy:
“I dwell in the high and holy place, with him also who is of a contrite and humble spirit,
to revive the spirit of the humble,
and to revive the heart of the contrite.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.
True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.
True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
I have seen his ways, and will heal him:
I will lead him also,
and restore comforts to him and to his mourners.
verses.explainer: God's comfort is not mere sympathy but active intervention -- He is coming to restore, redeem, and renew His people.

verses.text:
I create the fruit of the lips:
Peace, peace, to him who is far off and to him who is near,”
says Yahweh; “and I will heal them.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
But the wicked are like the troubled sea;
for it can’t rest, and its waters cast up mire and mud.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
“There is no peace”, says my God,
“for the wicked.”
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

explainer.summary: True fasting is not ritual deprivation but justice: feeding the hungry, sheltering the homeless, freeing the oppressed. When you do this, your light will break forth like the dawn.
explainer.contextInBook: This chapter directly challenges the idea that religion is primarily about personal piety rather than social justice.

wordStudies.translatedAs: Glory, weight, honor, splendor
wordStudies.meaning: Glory, weight, honor, splendor. Literally 'heaviness' -- God's glory is the weighty reality of His presence.
wordStudies.significance: The final section of Isaiah builds toward the revelation of God's glory to all nations in the new creation.

verses.text:
“Cry aloud, don’t spare.
Lift up your voice like a trumpet.
Declare to my people their disobedience,
and to the house of Jacob their sins.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Yet they seek me daily,
and delight to know my ways.
As a nation that did righteousness,
and didn’t forsake the ordinance of their God,
they ask of me righteous judgments.
They delight to draw near to God.
verses.explainer: Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.

verses.text:
‘Why have we fasted,’ say they, ‘and you don’t see?
Why have we afflicted our soul, and you don’t notice?’
“Behold, in the day of your fast you find pleasure,
and oppress all your laborers.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Behold, you fast for strife and contention,
and to strike with the fist of wickedness.
You don’t fast today so as to make your voice to be heard on high.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
Is this the fast that I have chosen?
A day for a man to humble his soul?
Is it to bow down his head like a reed,
and to spread sackcloth and ashes under himself?
Will you call this a fast,
and an acceptable day to Yahweh?
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
“Isn’t this the fast that I have chosen:
to release the bonds of wickedness,
to undo the straps of the yoke,
to let the oppressed go free,
and that you break every yoke?
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

crossReferences.connection: Jesus applied this passage to himself at the start of his ministry in the Nazareth synagogue.

verses.text:
Isn’t it to distribute your bread to the hungry,
and that you bring the poor who are cast out to your house?
When you see the naked,
that you cover him;
and that you not hide yourself from your own flesh?
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.
Righteousness in Isaiah encompasses both moral uprightness and social justice -- right relationships with God and with other people.
True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
and if you pour out your soul to the hungry,
and satisfy the afflicted soul:
then your light will rise in darkness,
and your obscurity will be as the noonday;
verses.explainer: The contrast between light and darkness is central to Isaiah's message: God's salvation brings light to those trapped in the darkness of sin and exile.

verses.text:
and Yahweh will guide you continually,
and satisfy your soul in dry places,
and make your bones strong;
and you shall be like a watered garden,
and like a spring of water,
whose waters don’t fail.
verses.explainer: True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.
True worship requires justice and righteousness, not just ritual. God's salvation is offered to all, but sin must be confronted honestly.

verses.text:
“If you turn away your foot from the Sabbath,
from doing your pleasure on my holy day;
and call the Sabbath a delight,
and the holy of Yahweh honorable;
and shall honor it,
not doing your own ways,
nor finding your own pleasure,
nor speaking your own words:
